title: Fabric Quality Control
subtitle: Fabric Quality Control
summary: Fabric quality control is essential in textile production. EVS provides high-speed, AI-powered automated fabric inspection to reduce waste and improve yield.
desc: Fabric quality control is essential in textile production. It ensures defects are detected early and fabric meets required standards. However, manual inspection is often slow, inconsistent, and dependent on human judgment.

EVS (Edraak Vision System) is a modern fabric quality control system built for high-speed, AI-powered automated fabric inspection. It uses high-resolution cameras and smart algorithms to scan fabric in real time, detect defects, and provide instant feedback during production.

Traditional inspection typically runs at 40–45 meters per minute, making it hard to maintain consistent quality. Defects are often missed, leading to higher waste and delays. Without a strong textile quality control system, efficiency and product quality are affected.

EVS solves this with an advanced fabric defect detection system that operates at speeds up to 150 meters per minute. As an industrial inspection system, it works across fabric types and integrates into production lines or looms for continuous monitoring and accurate detection.
When a defect is detected, the system logs and marks it instantly. This creates reliable data that helps teams take quick action and improve consistency in quality control in the textile industry.

All inspection data is available in a simple dashboard, showing defect patterns, fabric quality trends, and production performance. Reports can be easily exported for better planning and process improvement.

With high-speed inspection, real-time monitoring, and intelligent reporting, EVS improves fabric quality control. It reduces waste, lowers defect rates, improves fabric yield, and increases efficiency in modern textile manufacturing.

pageContent.text: Fabric Quality Control is a fundamental process in textile manufacturing, ensuring that fabric meets defined quality standards before reaching customers. One of the most widely used methods is the four-point system, which assigns penalty points based on defect size and severity. This standardized approach allows manufacturers to classify fabric quality consistently and compare different batches effectively. Fabric Quality Control using the four-point system helps in maintaining transparency between suppliers and buyers while reducing disputes. It also provides a measurable way to evaluate defects, making it easier to track performance over time. As production volumes increase, adopting structured Fabric Quality Control methods becomes essential for maintaining consistency, improving efficiency, and ensuring compliance with international textile quality standards.
Legacy Quality Control in Fabric Quality Control Systems

Legacy Fabric Quality Control systems rely heavily on manual inspection, where operators visually examine fabric for defects. This traditional Fabric Quality Control approach often leads to inconsistent results due to human fatigue, subjectivity, and varying skill levels. Inspectors typically record defects on paper, which makes data difficult to track, analyze, or integrate into modern systems. As production speeds increase, manual Fabric Quality Control struggles to keep up, resulting in missed defects and reduced inspection accuracy. Another major issue is high wastage caused by short fabric pieces, as defects are not managed efficiently during cutting. Overall, legacy Fabric Quality Control methods limit scalability, reduce efficiency, and increase operational costs, making them less suitable for modern high-speed textile manufacturing environments.

Digital Fabric Quality Control systems modernize inspection by replacing paper-based processes with structured digital data entry. In this approach, every defect is logged with details such as type, location, and severity, creating a reliable and searchable database. Digital Fabric Quality Control improves record keeping, enhances traceability, and enables real-time monitoring of production quality. This data can be easily shared across departments and integrated with ERP systems, improving coordination between quality control, production, and management teams. Most importantly, digital Fabric Quality Control generates data that is suitable for optimization and advanced analytics. By identifying recurring defects and patterns, manufacturers can take proactive actions to improve processes, reduce waste, and enhance overall product quality.

Camera-based Fabric Quality Control systems use high-speed vision technology to automate defect detection. These systems capture continuous images of fabric during production, ensuring 100% fabric coverage without relying on human inspection. Advanced algorithms analyze the images in real time, detecting defects with near-human accuracy while maintaining consistent performance. Unlike manual Fabric Quality Control, camera systems do not suffer from fatigue or inconsistency, making them ideal for high-speed production lines. Additionally, all inspection data and images are stored for traceability, enabling quick review and validation of quality. Camera-based Fabric Quality Control significantly reduces missed defects, improves inspection reliability, and supports scalable operations, making it a key component of modern textile quality management systems.
Optimization Systems in Fabric Quality Control

Optimization systems are a key advancement in Fabric Quality Control, using inspection data to improve fabric utilization and reduce waste. These systems analyze defect locations, roll lengths, and quality grades to generate efficient cutting plans. One of the major benefits of optimization in Fabric Quality Control is the elimination of short fabric pieces, which are common in traditional processes. By planning cuts strategically around defects, manufacturers can maximize usable fabric and minimize losses. Optimization systems can also incorporate business rules, customer requirements, and production constraints, making them highly adaptable. Overall, integrating optimization into Fabric Quality Control helps improve efficiency, reduce costs, and enhance profitability in textile manufacturing.
Labelling in Fabric Quality Control Systems

Labelling in Fabric Quality Control ensures defects are accurately marked on fabric for downstream processes. Automated systems place precise labels synchronized with fabric movement, enabling easy identification during cutting. This reduces errors, minimizes wastage, and ensures defective sections are handled properly, improving efficiency and maintaining consistent quality across production.
Width Measurement in Fabric Quality Control

Width measurement in Fabric Quality Control ensures consistent fabric dimensions during production. Real-time monitoring systems detect variations and trigger alerts when deviations occur. Maintaining accurate width control reduces material wastage, improves cutting precision, and ensures compliance with customer specifications, making it a critical parameter for overall fabric quality.
Color Sensor Integration in Fabric Quality Control

Color sensors in Fabric Quality Control automate the process of measuring fabric color directly on the production line. This eliminates manual sampling, reduces delays, and ensures consistent color across batches. Real-time monitoring allows quick adjustments, improving efficiency and maintaining strict quality standards required by modern textile manufacturing.
